sections.heading: RT
sections.content: {"role": "Radiation therapy (RT) is an integral part of combined modality treatment for esophageal cancer. It is used in the preoperative (neoadjuvant) setting combined with chemotherapy to improve survival and resectability, in the definitive (non-surgical) setting for patients who are not surgical candidates or decline surgery, and for palliation of symptoms (dysphagia, bleeding, pain). Postoperative RT is considered for patients with positive margins or high-risk features after surgery.", "principles": ["Treatment recommendations should be made by a multidisciplinary team including radiation oncologists.", "All available diagnostic studies (CT, EUS, endoscopy reports, FDG-PET/CT) should be reviewed to define target volumes.", "CT simulation and conformal treatment planning (3D-CRT or IMRT) are required. Proton beam therapy (PBT) may be considered in clinical settings where reduction in dose to organs at risk is required, ideally within a clinical trial.", "Patients should be treated in the supine position for reproducible setup.", "Immobilization devices are strongly recommended.", "Respiratory motion may be significant; 4D-CT planning or other motion management techniques should be used, and margins may be modified.", "Target volumes must carefully encompass disease while sparing uninvolved stomach for future reconstruction.", "Treatment interruptions or dose reductions for manageable acute toxicities should be avoided; aggressive supportive care is preferred.", "Patients should be monitored weekly with vital signs, weight, and blood counts during RT."], "dose_frameworks": [{"name": "Preoperative RT", "total_dose": "41.4-50.4 Gy", "dose_per_fraction": "1.8-2.0 Gy/day", "fractions": "23-28 fractions", "schedule": "Daily, Monday-Friday.", "indication": "For patients with resectable esophageal or EGJ cancer receiving preoperative chemoradiation."}, {"name": "Postoperative RT", "total_dose": "45-50.4 Gy", "dose_per_fraction": "1.8-2.0 Gy/day", "fractions": "25-28 fractions", "schedule": "Daily, Monday-Friday.", "indication": "For patients with positive margins or high-risk features after surgery."}, {"name": "Definitive RT", "total_dose": "50-50.4 Gy", "dose_per_fraction": "1.8-2.0 Gy/day", "fractions": "25-28 fractions", "schedule": "Daily, Monday-Friday.", "indication": "For patients receiving definitive chemoradiation (non-surgical candidates or those declining surgery). Non-surgical candidates should receive the higher dose range (50-50.4 Gy) as lower doses may not be adequate."}], "approaches": [{"name": "Preoperative chemoradiation", "dose_fractionation": "41.4-50.4 Gy in 1.8-2.0 Gy fractions", "concurrent_chemotherapy": "Preferred regimens: paclitaxel and carboplatin (category 1) or fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (category 1). Other recommended: fluorouracil and cisplatin (category 1).", "indication": "Resectable locoregional esophageal or EGJ cancer. Preferred for adenocarcinoma; also standard for SCC.", "key_trial": "CROSS trial (van Hagen et al., NEJM 2012): Preoperative chemoradiation with paclitaxel and carboplatin significantly improved OS (median 49 vs. 24 months; HR 0.657, P=0.003) and DFS compared to surgery alone in patients with resectable esophageal or EGJ cancers.", "toxicities": "Esophagitis, neutropenia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, weight loss. Postoperative complications (anastomotic leak, pneumonia) are not significantly increased compared to surgery alone."}, {"name": "Definitive chemoradiation", "dose_fractionation": "50-50.4 Gy in 1.8-2.0 Gy fractions", "concurrent_chemotherapy": "Preferred regimens: paclitaxel and carboplatin (category 1) or fluorouracil and oxaliplatin (category 1). Other recommended: fluorouracil and cisplatin (category 1).", "indication": "Unresectable locoregional disease (T4b or bulky lymphadenopathy) or patients who decline surgery.", "key_trial": "RTOG 85-01 (Cooper et al., JAMA 1999): Chemoradiation (fluorouracil and cisplatin + 50.4 Gy) significantly improved 5-year OS (20% vs. 0%) and median survival (14 vs. 9 months) compared to RT alone in patients with esophageal cancer.", "toxicities": "Esophagitis, neutropenia, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, weight loss, pneumonitis, stricture."}, {"name": "Palliative RT", "dose_fractionation": "Various short courses (e.g., 30 Gy in 10 fractions, 20 Gy in 5 fractions)", "concurrent_chemotherapy": "RT alone is typically used for palliation.", "indication": "Symptom control (dysphagia, bleeding, pain) in patients with advanced disease not candidates for curative therapy.", "key_trial": "TROG 03.01 (Penniment et al., Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol 2018): Palliative chemoradiation showed minimal improvement in dysphagia relief over RT alone with increased toxicity.", "toxicities": "Esophagitis, fatigue, nausea."}]}

timeline.title: Regimen
timeline.body: Preoperative: 41.4-50.4 Gy in 23-28 fractions; Postoperative: 45-50.4 Gy in 25-28 fractions; Definitive: 50-50.4 Gy in 25-28 fractions; Palliative: 30 Gy/10 fx or 20 Gy/5 fx.
